instruments.name: MASTCAM
instruments.desc: Twin mast cameras for wide-area survey. Filter by rock type — matches show as highlights. Tag a target before spending laser or drill time on it.

upgrade.name: INFRARED / MULTISPECTRAL
upgrade.desc: Stronger mineral contrast in the passive survey view.

help.summary: Visual classifier. Tags rocks for ChemCam and drill targeting.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [1] to select MastCam. Aim at a rock and hold [E] to scan. The scan takes approximately 2 seconds. A floating label confirms the tag. Repeat for at least 3 rocks before using ChemCam or the drill.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: ChemCam and the drill require a MastCam tag — untagged rocks cannot be analyzed or cored. This interlock is enforced by the instrument. Hematite and sulfate are rarer than basalt and worth prioritizing for downstream analysis.

images.alt: MastCam view showing a rock target with scan progress bar and the floating tag label after completion

instruments.hint: Set survey filter, pan the mast. Hold [E] on a rock to scan and tag. Scroll to zoom.

instruments.name: CHEMCAM
instruments.desc: Standoff laser spectroscopy: vaporizes a pin spot and reads elemental composition. Use after MastCam to judge if a rock is worth drilling.

upgrade.name: MULTI-SHOT BURST
upgrade.desc: 3 shots on different spots for averaged reading. Better accuracy.

help.summary: Laser spectrometer. Identifies rock composition at standoff distance.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: MastCam tag required first. Press [2] to select ChemCam. Aim at a tagged rock and hold [E] to fire. Keep aim steady — the sequence fires multiple pulses over several seconds. When the spectrum readout appears, acknowledge it to archive the result.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: ChemCam only fires on MastCam-tagged rocks — selecting an untagged target does nothing. ChemCam has a limited shot count before cooldown. Results are queued for UHF transmission automatically — no extra steps required.

images.alt: ChemCam spectrum readout panel showing elemental peaks and the acknowledge button

instruments.hint: MastCam-tagged rock in range. Hold [E] to fire IR; release to stop. Full pulse then integration — CHEM badge, SEE RESULTS for spectrum. Saved for SAM.

instruments.name: DRILL
instruments.desc: Rotary percussive bit on the arm turret. Collects powdered rock for the lab. Run MastCam and ChemCam on the target first for best results.

upgrade.name: BIT WEAR KIT
upgrade.desc: Reduces drill time on tagged rocks.

help.summary: Core sampler. Collects rock powder for SAM analysis.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Drive within 1.5m of a rock target. Press [3] to select the drill. Aim the arm with the mouse. Hold [E] to drill — the sample is collected and added to inventory automatically when complete. Nothing to pick up manually.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: Drilling is the highest power draw on the vehicle. After a drill cycle, open the RTG panel and activate the Power Shunt to recover the battery. The shunt disables driving while active — hold position. Targets analyzed by both MastCam and ChemCam yield significantly more science value from the sample.

images.alt: Drill arm extended toward a rock target with the drill progress indicator active

instruments.hint: Drive within 1.5m of target. Aim the arm with mouse. Hold [E] to drill and collect powder.

instruments.name: APXS
instruments.desc: Alpha particle X-ray spectrometer. Placed against rock or soil for bulk elemental chemistry. Slower than ChemCam but more accurate for light elements.

help.summary: Contact spectrometer. Elemental analysis by physical proximity.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Drive close to a rock target. Press [4] to select APXS. Hold [E] to initiate contact. A brief calibration sequence begins — follow the on-screen prompts. The integration runs automatically after calibration. Do not move the rover while APXS is integrating.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: APXS requires physical proximity — standoff analysis is not possible. It is slower than ChemCam but more accurate for light elements. A rock analyzed by both ChemCam and APXS yields significantly more science value than one analyzed by only one. Results are queued for transmission automatically.

images.alt: APXS instrument panel showing the contact calibration prompt and integration progress bar

instruments.hint: Drive within 1.5m of a rock. WASD aims the arm. Hold [E] to place sensor — 3s countdown, then detect surface elements. Trace elements on drill if analyzed.

chainBonuses.label: TRACE ELEMENTS
chainBonuses.description: Yields 1–2 surface trace elements on drill

instruments.name: DAN
instruments.desc: Fires neutrons into the ground and detects hydrogen. Maps subsurface water content while driving. A signal spike indicates ice or hydrated minerals.

upgrade.name: DEPTH EXTENDER
upgrade.desc: Scan depth from 0.5m to 1.0m below surface.

help.summary: Subsurface hydrogen detector. Finds ice and hydrated minerals while driving.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [5] to select DAN. Press ACTIVATE or [E] to enable passive scan. Drive normally — DAN scans automatically while moving. Watch the signal readout. When the return count spikes, stop immediately. Hold [E] for 4 seconds to initiate prospecting. Stay still for the full integration cycle.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: Driving away from a signal resets integration — stop the moment you see a spike. Once prospecting begins, the rover cannot move for the full cycle duration (approximately 2 Mars hours). Attempting to drive cancels the prospect. The spike may be subtle — watch the readout carefully while driving.

images.alt: DAN instrument panel showing the neutron return count readout with a visible signal spike
DAN prospecting phase active — rover stationary, integration timer running

instruments.hint: Draws ~10W on the main bus while running (see power HUD). ACTIVATE / STANDBY or [E] toggles; when on, billed while you drive. Future: heatmap + anomalies.

instruments.name: SAM
instruments.desc: Onboard analytical lab: gas chromatograph, mass spectrometer, tunable laser. Identifies organics, measures isotope ratios, characterizes molecular composition.

help.summary: Onboard lab. Runs chemical analysis on drill samples.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Drill a sample first — it enters inventory automatically. Press [6] to select SAM. Open the experiment panel, load the sample, and select a mode: Pyrolysis (organics and volatiles), Wet Chemistry (soluble minerals), or Isotope Analysis (elemental ratios). Confirm to queue. Acknowledge the readout when the experiment finishes.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: SAM draws 25W while an experiment is running — the highest passive draw on the vehicle. Monitor your power budget. Only one experiment runs at a time. The experiment processes in the background; you can drive while it runs. Do not let the RTG degrade while SAM is active.

images.alt: SAM instrument panel showing experiment mode selection and the active experiment progress bar

instruments.hint: Park at target. Drill with [E] — control pressure with mouse Y. Then wait for analysis.

instruments.name: RTG
instruments.desc: Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator. Converts plutonium-238 decay heat to electricity. The rover's only power source.

upgrade.name: HEAT EXCHANGER
upgrade.desc: Improves thermal efficiency. Faster charge rate.

help.summary: Primary power source. The only instrument where failure ends the mission.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Boot via the power HUD on startup. Monitor durability in this panel. OVERDRIVE temporarily boosts reactor output — all instruments lock out for approximately 19 seconds during and after the burst. POWER SHUNT immediately refills the battery but disables driving while the cycle is active.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: RTG at 0% durability ends the mission immediately — no recovery. Every other instrument can break and the mission continues in degraded state. The RTG cannot. Repair as soon as durability drops. Repair cost scales with damage level — early repair is always cheaper. Each repair slightly lowers the maximum durability ceiling.

images.alt: RTG instrument panel showing durability bar, OVERDRIVE and POWER SHUNT buttons

instruments.hint: OVERDRIVE: 2× speed, then instrument lockout. POWER SHUNT: fills battery, −50% load ~3h, no driving — 24h cooldown.

instruments.name: REMS
instruments.desc: Twin boom sensors on the mast: temperature, wind, pressure, humidity, UV. Provides continuous environmental monitoring and storm alerts.

help.summary: Weather station. Passive environmental monitoring with sample quality bonus.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [8] to select REMS. Press ACTIVATE or [E] to enable surveying. REMS runs passively at approximately 1W once on. Current conditions — pressure, temperature, wind speed, UV index — update continuously in the panel.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: When REMS is active and surveying, it applies a +10% sample quality bonus to all science collected within approximately 3 meters of the rover. Keep REMS running during any science work. It also provides dust storm level alerts — watch the REMS panel for incoming storm warnings before they affect instrument operation.

images.alt: REMS panel showing live pressure, temperature, wind, and UV readouts with surveying status active

instruments.hint: ~1W on the bus while surveying. ACTIVATE / STANDBY or [E] toggles (STANDBY saves power). +10% sample quality within 3m when on.

instruments.name: RAD
instruments.desc: Measures high-energy radiation: protons, heavy ions, neutrons, gamma rays. Monitors cumulative dose and alerts on solar particle events.

upgrade.name: PARTICLE SPECTROMETER
upgrade.desc: Identifies individual isotopes in cosmic ray flux.

help.summary: Radiation monitor. Tracks cumulative dose and alerts on solar events.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [9] to select RAD. Press ACTIVATE or [E] to enable monitoring. RAD runs passively at approximately 2W. Cumulative dose is tracked across the session and shown in the panel.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: High-radiation zones increase instrument durability decay rates — all instruments degrade faster under elevated radiation. RAD alerts you to solar particle events before they peak. Moving to lower-radiation terrain reduces passive damage to all instruments.

images.alt: RAD panel showing current radiation level, cumulative dose, and zone indicator

instruments.hint: ~2W on the bus while monitoring. ACTIVATE / STANDBY or [E] toggles. Tracks dose; storm alerts when on.

instruments.name: HEATER
instruments.desc: Warm Electronics Box heating system. Keeps internal temperature above survival thresholds. Competes with science instruments for power.

upgrade.name: INSULATION UPGRADE
upgrade.desc: Reduces heat loss rate by 30%. Less heater draw at cold sites.

help.summary: Thermal management. Automatic — protects instruments from cold damage.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [H] to open the thermal panel. The heater runs automatically — no manual activation. The thermostat engages below -10°C and shuts off above +5°C. Current rover internal temperature and ambient temperature are shown in the panel.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: The heater draws up to 12W when active — competing directly with science instruments for power. Colder landing sites mean more heater cycles and less power available for science. Monitor the thermal zone indicator: COLD reduces instrument efficiency, CRITICAL locks some instruments entirely.

images.alt: Heater panel showing internal temperature, ambient temperature, heater draw, and thermal zone status

instruments.hint: Automatic thermostat. Heater kicks in below -10°C, shuts off above +5°C. Colder sites = more power to survive = less for science. [H] or the HTR control under WHLS beside the power HUD.

instruments.name: LGA
instruments.desc: Omnidirectional low-gain antenna for direct-to-Earth communication. Primary command uplink and mission mailbox.

help.summary: Mission mailbox. Missions arrive here; completed results transmit from here.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [R] to open the LGA panel. Missions from Mission Control arrive as messages in your inbox. When a mission is complete and awaiting transmit, file results from this panel. Press ACTIVATE or [E] to toggle the antenna link.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: Check the LGA when unsure what to do next — new mission assignments appear here. The LGA has a low data rate and is not suitable for large science packages; use UHF for bulk science transmission. The DSN Archaeology upgrade (if installed) extends the receiver into legacy frequency bands.

images.alt: LGA panel showing the message inbox with an unread mission assignment

instruments.hint: ACTIVATE / STANDBY or [E] toggles. Receives mission messages and sends daily heartbeat. CHECK MAIL for incoming transmissions.

instruments.name: UHF
instruments.desc: High-bandwidth UHF antenna for relay via overhead orbiters (MRO, MAVEN). Primary science downlink.

upgrade.name: DUAL-BAND MODULE
upgrade.desc: Enables simultaneous uplink/downlink during passes. Halves transfer time.

help.summary: Science downlink. Transmits queued data automatically during orbital relay windows.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [T] to open the UHF panel. Press ACTIVATE or [E] to enable. All science results you collect are queued automatically — you do not select individual items. When an orbital relay window opens, the antenna transmits from the top of the queue. Science Points are awarded when Earth acknowledges receipt.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: Relay windows are on a fixed schedule — if the panel shows time to next pass, wait for it. Missing a window means waiting for the following orbit. Data transmits oldest-first. SP is awarded on acknowledgment, not on collection — data sitting in the queue does not count toward mission objectives.

images.alt: UHF panel showing orbital pass schedule, transmission queue length, and active uplink progress

instruments.hint: ACTIVATE / STANDBY or [E] toggles. Auto-transmits queued discoveries during orbital passes. Higher accuracy = faster uplink + lower power draw.

instruments.name: WHLS
instruments.desc: Rocker-bogie wheel motors and steering actuators. Power draw only active while the chassis is moving.

upgrade.name: EFFICIENCY MOTORS
upgrade.desc: Lower draw per meter; same top speed.

help.summary: Drive system. WASD to move; wheel condition affects speed.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Press [B] to open the wheels panel. WASD controls movement. Current drive speed and active modifiers are shown in the panel. Use REPAIR if wheel condition has degraded.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: Wheel condition degrades over time and from hazard events. Degraded wheels reduce movement speed. Power draw is only active while moving. Some operations lock the drive system temporarily — the RTG Power Shunt disables driving while active, and DAN prospecting immobilizes the rover for the full integration cycle.

images.alt: Wheels panel showing drive speed, wheel condition, and active speed modifiers

instruments.hint: Select with [B] (slot 13). WASD to drive. REPAIR restores traction hardware. UPGRADE track reserved for drive efficiency / tread packages.

instruments.name: MIC
instruments.desc: Rover-mounted microphone capturing Mars ambient sound. Audio sourced from NASA Perseverance recordings.

upgrade.name: HIGH-FIDELITY MIC
upgrade.desc: Wider frequency response captures faint geological sounds at greater distance.

help.summary: Ambient microphone. Layers wind, atmosphere, and storm audio.

sections.heading: OPERATION
sections.body: Select the MIC slot to open the panel. Press ACTIVATE or [E] to enable ambient audio. The microphone layers wind, atmospheric pressure, and storm intensity into the ambient soundscape.

sections.heading: WATCH FOR
sections.body: Audio reacts in real time to wind speed, time of day, and storm intensity — louder and more turbulent during dust storms. Approximately 1W passive draw while active.

instruments.hint: ~1W on the bus while listening. ACTIVATE / STANDBY or [E] toggles. Ambient audio reacts to wind speed, time of day, and storm intensity.
